mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 6 Series 640d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned N57 engine typically costs £4,500–£6,500 supply only for the pre‑LCI generation, with LCI units slightly higher. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,700. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your 640d.

It depends on how it has been maintained. The N57 is a powerful and refined engine, but it has known weaknesses — timing chain wear (particularly pre‑2015), main bearing issues, EGR clogging and oil leaks. With 5,000–8,000‑mile oil changes and preventative maintenance, it can be reliable; with 20,000‑mile BMW‑recommended intervals, it is a ticking time bomb.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 640d engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain wear (pre‑2015 models), main bearing/crankshaft issues, EGR valve and cooler clogging, turbocharger failures, oil leaks (valve cover, oil filter housing, turbo lines), and fuel injector problems.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a 640d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. A 6 Series 640d is a £8,000–£25,000 vehicle — a £5,000–£6,000 recon is 20–75% of the car's value. For lower‑value examples (sub‑£10,000), replacement may exceed our 70% BMW scrap threshold. Evaluate carefully based on your car's generation, mileage and overall condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 640d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 640d engine code is N57D30T1. Pre‑2015 and LCI (2015+) versions share the same code but benefit from revised timing chain design in later cars. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent the common failures on a 640d?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the single most important prevention is 5,000–8,000‑mile oil changes with BMW LL‑04 5W30 or 5W40 oil. Avoid prolonged idling, use premium diesel to limit EGR deposits, and have the timing chain inspected at 100,000 miles. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle or knocking, book an inspection immediately.

problems.title: Timing Chain Wear (Pre‑2015 Models)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking, delayed start, engine warning light, misfires.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught before chain snaps)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The N57's timing chain is prone to premature wear, particularly on pre‑2015 models. The chain, guides and tensioners weaken over time, causing excessive play and a metallic rattling when cold. If you hear a rattling noise on cold start, have the chain inspected immediately — ignoring it can lead to catastrophic engine damage. Many owners recommend replacing the chain preventatively around 100,000 miles. Regular oil changes every 5,000–8,000 miles are the single most effective prevention.

problems.title: EGR Valve and Cooler Clogging
problems.symptoms: Engine warning light, rough running, loss of power, coolant disappearing without visible leaks.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear

problems.recommendation: Carbon builds up in the EGR system over time, leading to performance dips and coolant leaks. This issue might seem minor at first, but a faulty EGR cooler can cause the engine to overheat — or even catch fire in extreme cases. If you notice any of these signs, have the system inspected immediately. Periodic cleaning of the EGR system every 40,000–60,000 miles is recommended.

problems.title: Main Bearing / Crankshaft Issues
problems.symptoms: Knocking or rumbling noise from the engine, particularly when cold; metallic noises when hot.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear knocking, stop driving and book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The N57's main bearings are a known weakness — they are marginal compared to the older M57. Insufficient oil pressure at low RPM (often with no warning lights) can accelerate bearing wear. Frequent oil changes (5,000–8,000 miles max) are essential. Some owners also recommend replacing the connecting rod bearings preventively if mileage exceeds 120,000 miles. If you hear a knocking noise, do not drive the car — bottom-end failure is catastrophic.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 640d? Check These First
buyIf: * Pre‑2015: timing chain replaced or inspected (or budget for it)
* No cold‑start rattle — walk away if you hear anything unusual
* Full service history with 5,000–8,000‑mile oil changes (not the 20,000‑mile BMW schedule)
* No oil leaks (valve cover, oil filter housing, turbo lines)
avoidIf: * Cold‑start rattle with no timing chain history — walk away
* Knocking or rumbling from the engine — potential bearing failure
* Poor service history — N57 engines are unforgiving of neglect
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — potentially expensive

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The N57 is a powerful and refined straight‑six diesel that delivers effortless performance in the 6 Series. But it has known weaknesses — timing chain wear, main bearing issues, EGR clogging and oil leaks. The key to longevity is aggressive maintenance: 5,000–8,000‑mile oil changes, not the BMW‑recommended 20,000‑mile intervals. Buy on full service history, and if you're looking at a pre‑2015 car, budget for timing chain replacement. A well‑maintained 640d is a rewarding, long‑lived grand tourer; a neglected one is a £5,000+ bill waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare 640d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 6 Series 640d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or ticking; timing chain wear indicator
* Knocking — Listen for knocking or rumbling; main bearing failure indicator
* Service History — Check oil change intervals (5,000–8,000 miles ideal; 20,000 miles is a red flag)
* Oil Leaks — Check under the car and around the engine; valve cover, filter housing and turbo lines are common leak points
* EGR System — Check for engine warning light or rough running; EGR clogging is common
* Turbo Health — Listen for whistling or whining; turbo failure indicator
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures
